sentences.text: Yeah.
In the meantime, I was commanding.
Our regimental commander, Colonel O'Brien, had come up and-- as well as General Frederick, who was the division commander.
He was a major general, Colonel O'Brien was a full colonel, I was a lieutenant colonel.

And we were taking inventory and my company commander actually went inside of the stockade, part of-- there were some large buildings right inside the gate that was used as a-- that's where they prepared the food for the inmates, what little they got, which was very little.
And they were all-- practically all of them were terribly emaciated.
And they had two barracks in there that were filled with sort of an infirmary, two infirmaries.
And almost all the people in there were dying or some were already dead.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And some of them had been dead for several days, the ones lying out.
So that the smell of death was just overpowering.
We were-- we were still having a difficult time [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] trying to comprehend.

We just couldn't-- it was hard to realize how those people have been treated-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we were still partly dazed, I'll say, at the-- the enormity of death there and the cruelty that went on there.
But it did quieten down and C Company came up and took over the guarding of the prisoners.
I then stayed there until about right at dark and saw that everything was fairly secure.
In the meantime, my battalion headquarters had moved up to the city of Dachau and established a headquarters there in a building in-- in the city.
And one of the men from headquarters came up and took me back to the headquarters.
I didn't know where it was.
And I just had gotten there when I got word that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was to relieve C Company [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] because the First Battalion would resume the attack in the morning.
My-- my battalion will not.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, I was a little unhappy about that.
But I didn't relieve C Company until we started that night-- and late that-- well, in the early morning.
And by early morning, I had-- I didn't put I Company back in, I took one of my other companies, L Company, and put it in the can.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I departed with what was left of the battalion.
Well, a short time after that, I got word that the 1st Battalion had run across another concentration camp, which it turned out was one of these slave labor camps.
It had about 8,000 people in it and they were in much better shape than the ones in Dachau, just a few miles from Dachau.
I was ordered to relieve the 1st Battalion so that they could continue the attack.
So I sent my K Company over to relive the 1st Battalion at that concentration camp.
That was on the 30th of April.
The morning we took-- the day we took the-- went-- first one into the camp was April 29, which was Sunday.
The following morning I-- I had resumed control of the camp with my L Company.

I send my K Company over to the new camp that the 1st Battalion had liberated and then I took what was left of the battalion, which was one company, I Company, and M Company, [INAUDIBLE] Company and had a headquarters into Munich and we established a command post.
Because the 1st Battalion went right into Munich with very little difficulty.
But on the morning of the 30th, the Germans were shot at that point.
They just weren't putting up any more resistance.
So I followed the First Battalion and had them set up a headquarters in the city of Munich.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Oh.
That morning, on the 30th, I got word that an artillery battalion from the Corps, Corps Army, would come up to relieve me that morning of the 30th.
Well, I had L Company in there so I pulled L Company out.
I took L Company in with me.
And this Army [INAUDIBLE] battalion commander came up and I explained the situation to him.
He took over the camp.
When I got into Munich and late that evening, I got word that I had to go back and relieve that artillery battalion.
By that time I'm furious.
Well, I said, I'm not going to be in a hurry about it.

Well, the next morning-- that would have been on May 1st, I guess-- I sent L Company back there, I went back with them, and when we took over the camp again and were there until May 3rd, when-- L Company was there until May 3rd, when a unit from our division, the 179th Infantry, took over the camp.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Well, that was about it as far as camp was concerned.
Yes.

The ones I spoke to-- it finally dawned on me after we'd been there a little while that we-- we-- we encountered several prisoners who were outside of a-- of a prison compound and they were in pretty good shape.
And it dawned on me these guys were what we might call trustees.
They work-- they did all the dirty work.
And talking to the prison who was with me, he explained to me that they did all the dirty work.
They're the ones that had to handle the bodies, put them in the crematorium.
And they got more-- better food than the ones inside.
I don't know how many there were, but there-- probably a dozen or more.
I saw four or five.
They were outside and they were all in pretty good physical shape as contrasted to the ones that were inside, who hardly anybody weighed more than 90 pounds, let's say, just skin and bone.
Well, this one guy, this was prisoner I had, took me around and showed me some aspects of the camp.
He showed me an area where there was a-- --some boards like latticework over a pile of-- over some sand.
And he got down and explained to me how the Germans executed-- not-- many German officers, he said.
Sometime before we arrived, they made them get down and put their heads down and they put a bullet in the back of their head.
Apparently--
Well, apparently, that was the residue of the attack on Hitler.
See, they didn't know who to hold responsible for that attack on Hitler so they just picked out a bunch of people who were on the staff of a-- of a general who was one of the plotters.
Some of them came to Dachau and they were executed there.
Or anybody that they thought was wanting to give up at the last minute were executed.
These were German officers.
They were the only ones, so the prisoner told me, that were executed in that manner.
There were a number of prisoners hung or executed in Dachau, but they were executed by hanging.
And just inside the compound was a small-- next to the kitchen area, there was a small compound that was a prison area within the prison.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anybody that violated the rules were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] picked up or if they tried to escape, they were put in this small room just inside the prison area and there they were beaten and tortured and-- and then generally were hung.

There was a place there with a small spike coming out of the cement wall, L-shaped, and there they would put the prisoners on a chair with a rope around their neck and then yank the chair out from under them and that's how they were hung.
We also found some pictures where they had hung them up by the hands to a pole or a tree.
Exquisite torture.
The Germans took too many pictures.
They like to photograph all the horrors that they engaged in.
Anyway, I got a pretty good bird's eye view from the trustees that-- that I call trustees that told me what had been going on in there.
But boy, I never went inside the compound.

My company commander did, he went in and got-- took in inventory of the kitchen to see if there was any food there, he and some of his men, trying to figure out how we were going to try to get them some food.
But I myself never went inside the compound.
The crematorium was out-- right outside of the compound.
There was a gas chamber there, but it-- and there was a shower, [GERMAN] bad or something like that they called it, [GERMAN] bad.
It had a sign on it which meant shower.
And there was another small cement structure there.
It was clearly marked gas.
I mean, it had a the skull and crossbones sort of like on it.
We immediately knew it was a gas chamber.
However, as far as any evidence about that gas chamber ever being used, there's not the slightest evidence that was ever used to kill anyone.
And it quite clearly was not, from what all the prisoners said.
And there were subsequent investigations indicated that that gas chamber was used only to fumigate clothing.
It was never used to exterminate people.
And I'm convinced that that was a fact.
There was a gas chamber some miles out of Munich, but that was used.
I think some old castle they called it.
I don't recall the name, but we found out that prisoners had been transported to there to be executed.
But Dachau was not an extermination camp per se, not like Auschwitz or Buchenwald, where they executed thousands.
The executions in Dachau probably numbered no more than 1,000 or 2,000.
Most of the people who died there died of disease.
Practically all the inmates in there had acute dysentery and many of them had typhus.
So they were just in-- they were-- well, to give you an example, the barracks that were in there were constructed to hold about somewhere around 250 inmates.
Boy, that was crowding it.
We got there, there was something like 1,500 crowded into those small structures.
So there were no-- sanitation was nonexistent.
And the resistance to any kind of disease like, pneumonia flu, whatever, it just did not exist.
So they died primarily of, I would say, severe malnutrition.
One of them-- I did talk with the prisoners and say, what did you get to eat?
Well, they said, we got a cup of coffee in the morning and a piece of bread in the evening.
This was at the last.
Apparently, years before they had treated them much better.
But in the last year of the war, Germany itself was running out of food so they were getting almost no-- nothing to eat at all and malnutrition was extreme.
They say hardly anyone weighed over 95 pounds.
And of course, the resistance went down and typhus was endemic.
And even after we-- in a couple of days, we had two Army hospitals before I left-- before my battalion left, two Army hospitals came up and they immediately started taking care of prisoners.
We did not have any responsibility except as guards.
Well, they immediately took over the care of the prisoners and they died at the rate of, I don't know, 50 to 75 a day for the first week or so after those two hospitals got there.
They were just too far gone.
They could did nothing for them. so they kept dying like 50, 75, 100 a day for about the first week until with food and medicine, most of them eventually recovered.
So the situation in there, the conditions were just beyond belief.
No sanitation, utter cruelty.
They were-- had what they call the roll call square.
That's where all the dead bodies were that we saw inside the camp when we first came up and that's where they assembled the prisoners.
If anybody tried to-- violated the rules or anything, everybody in the camp had to turn out.
For 24 hours they had to stand in that roll call square.
It's pretty cold in Munich and that area.
The temperature and the weather is much comparable to Denver here, snows in the wintertime and gets quite cold.
And they had nothing but thin clothing on.
Of course, in the process, many passed out, many died at that-- what they call the roll call square.
But that's it.
They would punish everybody in the camp if somebody broke the rules.
And to get back to these bodies that I saw being tossed through the air, we discovered that they call them capos, C-A-P-O.
I don't know where the name comes from.
Anyway, they call those capos.
And in each barracks, there was a German who was in charge of the barracks.
Now, he was a German prisoner.
You have to remember that Dachau was originally constructed to house Germans only.
That was the first concentration camp that Hitler had constructed right after he took power in 1933.
Well, that was to house German dissidents.
And [INAUDIBLE] they threw Catholic priests, particularly were the favorite target, and professors and people that they thought were enemies of the Nazi movement.
Well, that's what Dachau was for originally.
In the last year or so the war, they started throwing some ordinary criminals in there plus there was quite a resistance movement, or a small resistance movement, in Bavaria.
And many of those people were apprehended and they were put in Dachau.
And they were there when we got there.
But each barracks, there was a German who was in charge of it.
He was the capo.
And he's the one that reported any infractions of the rules.
And when he reported somebody, then they were tortured and very often executed.
So that's the reason the prisoners hated them.
They killed them all with their bare hands and I suspect with boards and whatever.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It-- and then we find the evidence of gruesome medical experiments from Dr.
Schilling and one other doctor [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] performed experiments on-- Rasher, it was Dr.
Rasher performed gruesome medical experiments on the people.
Usually, all the experiments ended up in a rather agonizing death.
And they had pictures of them.
Yes.
Well, they were used in the war crimes trial.
Germans were great picture takers.
And I remember they had pictures of a-- one prisoner they had in some kind of pressure chamber and they would simulate different altitudes.
This is for the Luftwaffe, the German Air Force, apparently, to see how-- at what altitude they would die.
And they had them in these pressure chambers and it showed a man gradually dying as they reduced the pressure.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The human body literally explodes when they get to zero-- down to zero pressure.
And then the other experiment he was conducting for the Luftwaffe was to see how people could-- how long they could exist in cold water.
So they'd start out at degrees like 40 degrees and strip them naked and then get down to 30 degrees and see how long they lasted at various temperatures.
Presumably, this was for the German Air Force in case they ditched out over the sea.
So those were agonizing deaths, of course.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There were those two doctors who performed I think practically all of the experiments there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were all kinds of pictures of brutality at the-- like people hanging up to a tree by their hands.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They-- some of them took great delight-- some of the German SS troops took great delight in that torture.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] shortly after, a day or so after we had taken the camp [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this-- General Lyndon started [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] throwing his weight around.
And our commanding general down to my headquarters one morning, it was about a day or two-- two days, I think, after we had taken the cab.
He said, well, things are heating up here for you.
He said, you know, Lyndon is causing a big-- big stink and they're appoint an IG to the 7th Army to make an investigation.
He said, I'm going to send you on home.
He said, our division has been selected to participate in the invasion of Japan.

We're going back to the States and we're going to retrain and reequip and then we'll be part of the invasion forces for Japan and you'll be on-- you'll go home and take a-- get a leave and we'll be going back to the States right away and you can rejoin us in the States.
So I was a little unhappy about that.
But he said, I'll send a command car down for you tomorrow morning and you can-- you can take that command car and take whatever people you want and report to Le Havre, France, which was a port.
I was to be assigned to the Assembly Area Command which operated the ports, which is an American Army organization.
They call it the Assembly Area Command.
The purpose was to get troops back to the States.
That was the reason for that headquarters.
So the next morning, I-- I left [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] taking several of my men.
And it took us-- the roads were in terrible shape and clogged up, and it took us two or three days to make it to Le Havre, which was 500, 600 miles away.
And I reported in there one morning, we spent the night in Paris and we went Le Havre the next morning, got there before noon.
And I checked in with the Transportation Office at Le Havre.
The sergeant had a little office at the end of the dock there.
And said, I'm Colonel Sparks and I think I have orders here to be sent back to the States.
And he looked me over and he said, well, colonel, I'll have to call my lieutenant.
So he got on the phone there and in two or three minutes there came a lieutenant, first lieutenant, MP, military police officer.
He said, colonel, I'm sorry, but I've got orders to take you back to 7th Army Headquarters.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, I was in no mood to be taken anywhere.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So I said, well, look around you, lieutenant, and my men raised their rifles.
I said, I'll go back to 7th Army Headquarters but you're not going to take me.
Will you give me your word, he said.
I said, yes.
I give you my word.
So we left and went back to Paris and got a little drunk that night, I think.
And I think we spent a couple of days in Paris.
And then I took off back to where I came from.
I found 7th Army Headquarters was near Augsburg, which is in Bavaria.
It was in a small town near Augsburg.
When I got there and reported in, I was told that the 7th Army Headquarters was being disbanded, that the 3rd Army was taking over.
The 7th Army was going to be reconstituted in Austria, going to be occupation troops in Austria, and that I would report to General Patton at 3rd Army Headquarters in Augsburg.
Augsburg is quite a large city not far from Munich.
So I found the 7th Army Headquarters that evening in a very large building that looked like a municipal building and reported in.
And they said, well, General Patton's not here, come back in the morning.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So I found quarters for me and my me that night and the next morning about nine o'clock, I went back.
And I was told by the chief of staff that General Patton who was out and would not be back till sometime after lunch. he said, you go have lunch and you come back at one o'clock.
Well, I came back at one o'clock and I waited a little while and somebody came out and said, OK, General Patton will see you now.
So I went into one of the room-- a room that he had, a small room.
It had a couple of chairs and a desk.
And I saluted.
And he said, well, sit down, colonel.
And he said, well, he said, colonel, he said-- I am very-- I have some very serious charges against you and some of your men.
Well, I said, general, I'd like a chance to explain.
I don't know what the charges are, but I'd like to have a chance to explain.
He said, didn't you serve under me in Africa and Sicily?
And I said, yes, I did, general.
He said, well, you've been a damn fine soldier.
He said, I don't need any explanation.
He said, you go on back to your unit.
The whole interview lasted no more than a minute or two minutes, maybe.
So I saluted and left and went back to my Munich-- my unit.
Then I found out the inspector general, in the meantime, was conducting a very thorough investigation.
What a strange thing, nobody-- when the report came out that I found just a few years ago, it said that I was not available.
That's not true.
I was not there the first few days, but I came back and was back a good 10 days.
I was available all the time, but nobody ever said hello or goodbye to me.
And after I'd been back about 10 days, and I got orders then to report to Rheims, to [INAUDIBLE] headquarters, and that's what I did.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then from there, I came on back to the States, got back in August of 1945.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But I was always curious as to what happened in that investigation.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was told from various sources that the investigator-- that Patton had stopped the investigation and ordered the whole thing dismissed.
Well, I didn't know.
But as the years went on, none of us who were at Dachau ever talked much about it until they started this Holocaust Memorial week, various states did.
And then I begun to hear of claims by the 42nd Infantry Division that they were the first in Dachau and the ones who liberated it.
Well, that surprised me considerably.
I knew that they had been there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And at that time, then I started my own investigation of trying to find out what happened.
There were many things that happened in Dachau, obviously, that I did not know about because in a large area like that with all the buildings, it was-- my vision was always limited.
So I knew that things had gone on there that I did not know about.
But I wasn't sure what.
But I finally had a good friend who was an inspector general in the Army, a colonel, had retired.
And a few years ago, I asked him to see if he could dig out all the records that are available.
And he knew his way around in Washington.
And anybody trying to find anything in the United States Archives with the hundreds of millions of documents, it's a daunting task.
He started it anyway and spent several days at that job.
And he discovered then and prepared for me the most complete record of Dachau that has ever been compiled, this huge book that he got records from archives.
And there finally was the long missing investigation.
